questions.question: Explain the three methods involved in the Swing painting mechanism: paintComponent(), paintBorder(), and paintChildren(). What is the role of the paint() method as the container?
questions.hint: Think of the paint() method as a manager that delegates specific painting tasks to child methods
keyPoints: paint() is the container method that invokes paintComponent(), paintBorder(), and paintChildren()
paintComponent() is overridden to perform custom drawing; it should only paint the component itself
paintBorder() draws the component's border (if any)
paintChildren() paints all contained child components in correct Z-order
Following this sequence ensures proper layering of graphics

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Graphics class in Swing painting? Mention at least four important methods of the Graphics class used for drawing.
questions.hint: Remember Graphics class is passed as parameter to paintComponent() method
keyPoints: Graphics is an abstract class that provides methods for rendering graphical shapes
drawLine() - draws a line between two points
drawRect()/fillRect() - draws/fills rectangles
drawOval()/fillOval() - draws/fills ovals and circles
drawString() - renders text
setColor() and setFont() - customize appearance

questions.question: Explain the concept of lightweight components in Swing. How do lightweight components differ from heavyweight AWT components in terms of painting?
questions.hint: Consider how lightweight components share the same painting surface
keyPoints: Lightweight components do not have their own native window (peer)
They are painted entirely by Java code, not by the underlying OS
Lightweight components can have transparent backgrounds
They are painted in Z-order within their container
Heavyweight components have OS peers and are always opaque

questions.question: What is the difference between repaint() and update() methods in Swing? When is each method typically used?
questions.hint: Swing overrides update() to call paintComponent() directly without erasing background
keyPoints: repaint() requests a repaint and is safe to call from any thread
update() is called by the AWT painting system and erases background before painting
In Swing, repaint() ultimately calls paint() with proper double buffering
Directly calling update() can cause flickering in Swing applications
repaint(0, x, y, w, h) can request repaint for a specific region only

questions.question: Explain double buffering in Swing. How does Swing implement double buffering to reduce flickering?
questions.hint: Think of it as painting on paper first, then showing the final result
keyPoints: Double buffering draws to an off-screen image first, then copies to screen
This prevents user from seeing partially drawn frames
Swing enables double buffering automatically via RepaintManager
The entire component is drawn off-screen, then swapped to display
Results in smoother visual updates and eliminates flickering

questions.question: What is the proper way to override paintComponent() in a Swing component? List at least three best practices.
questions.hint: The super.paintComponent() call is crucial for proper background and UI rendering
keyPoints: Always call super.paintComponent(g) as first statement to maintain look-and-feel
Never call paintComponent() directly; use repaint() instead
Perform all custom drawing after calling super.paintComponent()
Use Graphics2D for advanced rendering (casting from Graphics)
Do not modify component state inside paintComponent() as it may be called unexpectedly

questions.question: Explain the complete painting mechanism in Swing with the sequence of method calls when a component needs to be repainted. Include the role of RepaintManager in your explanation.
questions.hint: Draw a flow diagram showing the hierarchy from repaint() to actual painting
keyPoints: User action or program triggers repaint() request
repaint() schedules a paint operation with RepaintManager
RepaintManager collects multiple repaint requests into single paint operation
RepaintManager calls update() on the component
update() (overridden in Swing) calls paint() which delegates to paintComponent(), paintBorder(), paintChildren()
Double buffering is applied automatically by Swing
All painting occurs on Event Dispatch Thread (EDT)

questions.question: Compare the painting mechanism in AWT versus Swing. Discuss how the difference in heavyweight and lightweight components affects rendering, transparency, and Z-ordering.
questions.hint: The key difference lies in who does the painting - OS or Java runtime
keyPoints: AWT uses heavyweight components with native OS peers; Swing uses lightweight components
Heavyweight components are always opaque; lightweight can be transparent
Heavyweight components are painted by OS; lightweight by Java
Heavyweight components appear above all lightweight components (Z-order issue)
Swing components can be layered using different Z-ordering strategies
AWT update() erases background causing flicker; Swing overrides to prevent this
Swing provides pluggable look-and-feel that AWT lacks

questions.question: Explain how the Model-View-Controller (MVC) architecture is applied in Swing components and its impact on rendering. How does this separation facilitate custom painting?
questions.hint: In Swing, the component itself acts as both View and Controller
keyPoints: MVC separates data (Model), display (View), and logic (Controller)
Swing combines View and Controller into UI delegate for each component
Model stores data, View observes model for changes
When model changes, it fires PropertyChangeEvents
UI delegate listens and triggers repaint() when needed
This allows custom rendering by overriding paintComponent()
Pluggable look-and-feel works because UI delegate can be swapped

questions.question: Explain the concept of the painting containment hierarchy in Swing. How does the order of painting affect the visual appearance when components overlap?
questions.hint: The order of component addition determines Z-order in the painting hierarchy
keyPoints: Components are organized in a containment hierarchy (Container -> children)
paint() method paints the component, then its border, then all children
Children are painted in the order they were added
Later-added components appear on top of earlier ones
Opaque components cover components behind them
Transparent components allow background to show through
Using setOpaque(false) creates transparency effect

questions.question: Discuss the three Swing look-and-feel implementations: Metal (Java), Nimbus, and System (Native). How does each affect the painting mechanism and component rendering?
questions.hint: All three use the same painting infrastructure but with different UI delegates
keyPoints: Metal (Ocean theme) - cross-platform Java look, painted entirely by Java code
Nimbus - modern LookAndFeel with vector-based rendering, uses Synth XML files
System - uses native OS look (Windows, GTK, Aqua)
Painting is delegated to UI delegates (ComponentUI) specific to each L&F
UIManager.setLookAndFeel() switches entire application appearance
Each UI delegate overrides paint() methods for custom rendering
Pluggable L&F allows visual customization without code changes

questions.question: Write a Java program to create a custom JPanel that draws a house (rectangle body + triangle roof) using the Graphics class. Include code showing proper overriding of paintComponent().
questions.hint: Triangle roof requires three (x,y) coordinate pairs for polygon
keyPoints: Extend JPanel class
Override paintComponent(Graphics g) with super.paintComponent(g) call
Use drawRect() for house body
Use drawPolygon() or drawLine() for triangular roof
Use setColor() to set fill colors
Use fillPolygon()/fillRect() for filled shapes
Add the panel to a JFrame and set visible

questions.question: Design a Swing application that displays a bouncing ball animation. Explain how Timer and paintComponent() work together to create smooth animation.
questions.hint: The Timer interval determines animation speed; 30-60 fps is typical
keyPoints: Create custom JPanel with ball position variables (x, y)
Use javax.swing.Timer for animation loop
Timer calls actionPerformed() which updates ball position
Call repaint() after position update
paintComponent() clears background and draws ball at new position
Use boundary checking to reverse ball direction
Double buffering in Swing ensures smooth animation without flicker

questions.question: Write a program to create a gradient background panel using Graphics2D. Demonstrate the use of GradientPaint class and explain the coordinate system in Swing painting.
questions.hint: Graphics2D provides enhanced 2D rendering capabilities over Graphics
keyPoints: Cast Graphics to Graphics2D: Graphics2D g2d = (Graphics2D) g
Create GradientPaint with two points and two colors
Set paint on Graphics2D: g2d.setPaint(gradient)
Fill rectangle covering entire panel for background
Swing coordinate system: (0,0) is top-left corner
Positive X extends right, positive Y extends down
Width and height obtained from getWidth() and getHeight()

questions.question: Create a custom progress indicator using Swing. Design a component that displays a circular progress bar showing percentage completion. Explain the painting logic.
questions.hint: drawArc() takes start angle and arc angle in degrees; 0 degrees is at 3 o'clock
keyPoints: Create custom class extending JComponent
Store progress value (0-100) as instance variable
Override paintComponent() method
Draw outer circle using drawArc() method
Calculate arc angle based on progress percentage
Draw filled arc from 0 to progress angle using fillArc()
Use setStroke() for line thickness
Draw text showing percentage using drawString()
Call super.paintComponent() and use Graphics2D for better rendering
